title: Ordering relation_type names read opposite to the parent/child convention (task_depends_on_task footgun) — backwards edges file silently
description: relation_type canonical_ids use source_verb_target naming, so task_depends_on_task reads as parent(source) depends_on child(target). But the closure-table convention + the current-state deriver treat parent as the PREREQUISITE and child as the DEPENDENT — the child is blocked until the parent completes (context-sdk.ts:667-722). Name and stored direction are therefore opposite: the name implies the parent is the depender; the system treats the parent as the depended-upon. append-relation does no semantic direction check, so a backwards ordering edge is accepted silently and only surfaces in derived state — it just produced a backwards TASK-004/TASK-005 dependency, corrected at 5621c2a.

evidence.reference: edge-direction contract: {parent: D, child: T} => T blocked until D completed (parent=prerequisite, child=dependent) — opposite to the source_verb_target name reading
relation_type task_depends_on_task display_name 'depends on task', category ordering — name reads 'parent depends on child' while the deriver treats parent as prerequisite
the corrected dependency edge (5621c2a): original parent=TASK-005/child=TASK-004 was filed per the name, wrong per the deriver

impact: Any ordering relation whose verb implies the parent is the actor while the deriver treats parent as the prerequisite is easy to mis-direct; append-time silence means the error is invisible until topo/blocked derivation. Affects dependency-ordering correctness across tasks and any similarly-shaped ordering relation (e.g. phase_depends_on).
proposed_resolution: Carry explicit endpoint-role metadata on the relation_type (which endpoint is the prerequisite vs the dependent) and have appendRelation / append-relation validate or normalize an ordering edge's direction against it at write time, so name and stored direction cannot diverge and a backwards edge is rejected (or auto-oriented) at append rather than discovered in derived state. Extends the existing relation-type endpoint-kind metadata; the deriver already consumes the contract, so the fix makes the WRITE path enforce the same contract.
